explicit_text: My name is Jerry Bitz, born June 26, 1941, in Portland, Oregon. I started working on the waterfront as a casual in 1959 and became a registered A longshoreman in 1968. I was involved with ILWU Locals 8 and 40, serving as president of both at different times, and worked on the waterfront for over 40 years. My experiences ranged from driving cranes to handling difficult cargo like hides, and I was deeply involved in union politics starting in 1978. I feel blessed to have been part of the ILWU family and the waterfront community.
implicit_text: My name is Jerry, born June 26, 1941, in the largest city of Oregon. I started working on the waterfront as a casual in 1959 and became a registered longshoreman in 1968. I was involved with two local unions in that city, serving as president of both at different times, and worked on the waterfront for over 40 years. My experiences ranged from driving cranes to handling difficult cargo like hides, and I was deeply involved in union politics starting in 1978. I feel blessed to have been part of the waterfront union family and community.

explicit_text: The International Longshore and Warehouse Union, or ILWU, has been a central part of my life and work on the waterfront. I joined the union as a casual in 1959 and became a registered longshoreman in 1968. The ILWU fostered a strong sense of camaraderie among workers and provided protections and benefits that improved over time. I served in leadership roles within the union, including president of Locals 8 and 40, and participated actively in union conventions and politics. The union's history, including strikes and negotiations, shaped the waterfront and our families profoundly.
implicit_text: The waterfront union has been a central part of my life and work on the docks. I joined as a casual in 1959 and became a registered longshoreman in 1968. This union fostered a strong sense of camaraderie among workers and provided protections and benefits that improved over time. I served in leadership roles within the union, including president of two local chapters, and participated actively in conventions and politics. The union's history, including strikes and negotiations, shaped the waterfront and our families profoundly.

explicit_text: I was born in Portland, Oregon, in 1941 but actually grew up in Shorewood, a suburb about 18 miles south. Portland was the hub of my work life, especially on the waterfront where I joined the ILWU and worked for decades. The Portland union hall was a central place for meetings and conventions, and I remember attending union events there. The city and its waterfront have a rich history of labor struggles and community that I was proud to be part of.
implicit_text: I was born in Oregon’s largest city in 1941 but actually grew up in a suburb about 18 miles south. That city was the hub of my work life, especially on the waterfront where I joined the union and worked for decades. It and its waterfront have a rich history of labor struggles and community that I was proud to be part of.

explicit_text: Though I was born in Portland, I actually grew up in Shorewood, Oregon, which is about 18 miles south of Portland. Every day as a casual worker on the waterfront, I would commute from Shorewood into Portland hoping to get a job. It was a daily routine that shaped my early experiences with the waterfront and the union. Shorewood was home, but my work life was centered in Portland.
implicit_text: Though I was born in the largest city of Oregon, I actually grew up in a suburb about 18 miles south. Every day as a casual worker on the waterfront, I would commute from that suburb into the city hoping to get a job. It was a daily routine that shaped my early experiences with the waterfront and the union. That suburb was home, but my work life was centered in the bigger city.

explicit_text: My family lived through the 1948 West Coast Waterfront Strike when I was about 7 years old. My father was a longshoreman working nights on Gang 25, and during the strike, we struggled with very few benefits. I remember our church brought us food baskets, which humbled me as a kid because we weren't used to accepting charity. That strike was a tough time but it showed the strength and solidarity of waterfront families and the union.
implicit_text: My family lived through the major West Coast waterfront strike in 1948 when I was about 7 years old. My father was a longshoreman working nights on Gang 25, and during the strike, we struggled with very few benefits. I remember our church brought us food baskets, which humbled me as a kid because we weren't used to accepting charity. That strike was a tough time but it showed the strength and solidarity of waterfront families and the union.

explicit_text: I attended Tigard High School and graduated in 1960. It was near where I lived in the suburbs south of Portland. After high school, I went straight into waterfront work rather than military service. My time at Tigard was just before I began my long career with the ILWU and the waterfront community.
implicit_text: I attended the high school in a suburb south of Oregon’s largest city and graduated in 1960. It was near where I lived in the suburbs south of the big city. After high school, I went straight into waterfront work rather than military service. My time at that school was just before I began my long career with the waterfront union and community.

explicit_text: During my waterfront career, I had the opportunity to drive cranes at Schnitzer Steel Industries, particularly on scrap. Although I never took a formal crane operator plug, I learned by working alongside experienced operators and stepping in when needed. Schnitzer was one of the places where I expanded my skills beyond typical longshore work, enjoying the variety and challenge of different cargo types.
implicit_text: During my waterfront career, I had the opportunity to drive cranes at a major scrap metal company, particularly on scrap. Although I never took a formal crane operator plug, I learned by working alongside experienced operators and stepping in when needed. That company was one of the places where I expanded my skills beyond typical longshore work, enjoying the variety and challenge of different cargo types.

explicit_text: In 1978, I attended an ILWU convention in San Diego as a caucus delegate, which was quite an adventure for me. I was impressed by how the Hawaiian delegates hosted their members with a hospitality room that operated 24/7, showing strong union solidarity and care. That experience deepened my interest in union politics and leadership, which I pursued in the years following.
implicit_text: In 1978, I attended a waterfront union convention in a southern California city as a caucus delegate, which was quite an adventure for me. I was impressed by how the Hawaiian delegates hosted their members with a hospitality room that operated 24/7, showing strong union solidarity and care. That experience deepened my interest in union politics and leadership, which I pursued in the years following.

explicit_text: While I didn't speak much directly about Harry Bridges in my interview, his legacy as a founder and leader of the ILWU was always present in our union culture. His leadership influenced the union's approach to solidarity, strikes, and workers' rights, which shaped my own experiences on the waterfront. The union's history, including the strikes and political battles he led, was part of the backdrop to my career.

explicit_text: Although I did not discuss the 1971 West Coast Longshore Strike in detail, it was part of the broader history of labor struggles that shaped the ILWU and waterfront working conditions during my career. These strikes influenced union contracts, benefits, and the solidarity among longshoremen that I experienced firsthand. The strike history was always a reminder of the hard-fought gains we enjoyed later on.

explicit_text: The Taft–Hartley Act was part of the legal and political environment affecting the ILWU and waterfront labor relations during my time. While I didn't mention it directly, the act's restrictions on union activities influenced how we organized and negotiated. Understanding its impact was important in union leadership roles I held, especially during arbitration and contract discussions.

explicit_text: The Pacific Maritime Association was the employer organization we negotiated with on the waterfront. Throughout my career, especially during my time as president of Locals 8 and 40, I dealt with the PMA in arbitrations and contract talks. They represented the shipping companies and terminal operators, so our relationship was often contentious but essential to securing good working conditions for longshoremen.
implicit_text: The employer organization we negotiated with on the waterfront represented shipping companies and terminal operators. Throughout my career, especially during my time as president of two local unions, I dealt with this association in arbitrations and contract talks. Our relationship was often contentious but essential to securing good working conditions for longshoremen.

explicit_text: The Portland union hall was a central place for me and other ILWU members. It was where we gathered for meetings, conventions, and to organize our work. I remember going there with my dad and later attending conventions and caucuses there as a delegate and leader. The hall was a hub of union activity and community in Portland.
implicit_text: The union hall in Oregon’s largest city was a central place for me and other waterfront union members. It was where we gathered for meetings, conventions, and to organize our work. I remember going there with my dad and later attending conventions and caucuses there as a delegate and leader. The hall was a hub of union activity and community in that city.

explicit_text: Local 8 was my primary union local for 33 years as a longshoreman. I served in various leadership roles there, including president and business agent, and was deeply involved in labor relations and arbitration. Local 8 represented Portland longshoremen, and being part of it was like being part of a family with strong solidarity and shared history. It was where I learned much about union politics and worker advocacy.
implicit_text: One local union was my primary chapter for 33 years as a longshoreman. I served in various leadership roles there, including president and business agent, and was deeply involved in labor relations and arbitration. This local represented longshoremen in Oregon’s largest city, and being part of it was like being part of a family with strong solidarity and shared history. It was where I learned much about union politics and worker advocacy.

explicit_text: Later in my career, I worked for about nine or ten years with Local 40 as a clerk and supercargo. I also served as president of Local 40 at one point. This local represented clerks and other waterfront workers, and my role there expanded my experience beyond longshoreman duties. Local 40 was an important part of my union leadership journey and waterfront career.

explicit_text: Larry Clark was mentioned briefly in the context of union activities, but I did not elaborate much on my relationship with him. He was part of the ILWU leadership or membership during my time, and like many others, contributed to the union's strength and operations. Our shared commitment to the waterfront and union goals connected us in the broader ILWU family.
